title: The Favorite Bookshelf Tower
summary: A tall narrow bookshelf tower with applied crown moulding, a header cap, and slatted side legs — dressed-up trim details on a simple 1x12 box.
description:
This tower's box is a straightforward 1x12 case — two sides, four shelves, and a top — but its finished look comes almost entirely from the trim wrapped around that plain box afterward: side legs, a header, front bottom trim, and a crown moulding detail at the very top. Build the plain box first exactly like any simple bookshelf: sides, shelves, and top, joined square before any trim goes on.

**The side legs run the full height of the piece and are what give it its tower proportions** — 1x2 and 1x3 boards applied to the front and sides of the case, with the header trim sized and cut to fit your actual assembled box rather than to a fixed number, since real lumber width varies enough to throw off a fixed measurement on a piece this tall and narrow.

The crown moulding pieces at the top are genuinely the last detail, cut and measured to fit after everything else is square and trimmed — a small cove moulding kept modest so it reads as a finished cap rather than an ornate flourish. The back panel is optional, and going without it is a legitimate choice if the piece will sit against a wall where the back is never seen.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood or MDF, half sheet (optional back)
Primer and paint, or interior wood stain

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood or MDF, half sheet (optional back)
cutList.note: Optional; measure and cut to fit.

steps.title: Cut the case and leg parts
steps.body: Every case part keeps the board's full width, so only the lengths change. From the three 8 ft 1x12s cut two sides 68" long, five bottom-and-shelf pieces 22-1/2", and one top 24"; those eight pieces pack into exactly three boards allowing 1/8" for each saw cut, so cut the two 68" sides first and take the shelves from what is left. From the four 8 ft 1x3s cut four legs at 68-3/4" — one per board — then take the two 11-1/4" header sides and the 25-1/2" header top from the offcuts. From the 1x2s cut two side legs at 68-3/4" and hold the rest of that stock back, since the side trim, front bottom trim and crown are all measured off the assembled piece rather than cut to a number. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Stand the two 68" sides on edge and set the five 22-1/2" shelves between them, so the case comes to 24" wide overall — 22-1/2" plus 3/4" of side stock at each end. The 24" top then caps over both sides flush, and the optional back panel at 24" x 68-3/4" confirms that reading. Put the bottom shelf flush with the bottom ends of the sides, then space the remaining four wherever you want your books to sit, marking both sides together so every shelf lands level. Glue each joint and drive 2" brad nails through the outside face of each side into the shelf ends, nailing the top on last. Measure both diagonals across the open front before the glue sets — on a case 68-3/4" tall and only 24" wide a small rack shows badly.

steps.title: Add the header
steps.body: The header is the band that wraps the top of the case just under the crown, and it is fitted to your own piece rather than to a number. The two header sides are the case depth, 11-1/4" as drawn, and the header top spans the full trimmed front, which comes to 25-1/2" once the 3/4" side legs are on. Hold each piece against the case and mark it in place instead of measuring twice. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads into the case sides and top, keeping the header's top edge flush with the top of the case so the crown has a flat landing.

steps.title: Attach the side legs
steps.body: The legs are what give this piece its tower proportions, and all six are 68-3/4" — the full case height including the 3/4" top. Glue and nail a 1x3 to each front corner so its 2-1/2" face covers the case's front edge, then run a 1x3 and a 1x2 down each side so the applied stock adds 3/4" per side and brings the outside width to 25-1/2", which is exactly why the header top is cut 25-1/2". Keep the top ends of all six legs flush with the top of the case and start from the top, letting any length error fall at the floor where it will not show. Use 1-1/4" brads into the 3/4" case sides so nothing punches through the inside.

steps.title: Add the top and bottom side trim
steps.body: The two 7-1/4" 1x2 pieces fill the short gaps left on the sides between the applied legs. Measure the actual opening on your own piece and cut to that — the 7-1/4" in the cut list assumes the legs landed exactly where drawn, and yours will be a little different. Cut each a whisker long and pare it back until it presses in without forcing. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads, keeping the outside face flush with the legs on either side so the whole side reads as one plane.

steps.title: Add the front bottom trim
steps.body: The front bottom trim closes the front of the case at the floor, between the two front legs. Measure the actual gap between your legs and cut to that number; the 20-1/2" in the cut list is a starting value and the cut list itself marks it measure-and-cut-to-fit. Hold it with its bottom edge flush with the bottom of the case and its face flush with the legs, then glue it and pin with 1-1/4" brads into the front edge of the bottom shelf.

steps.title: Attach the back panel (optional)
steps.body: The back is optional, and skipping it is a fair choice if the tower will always stand against a wall. If you fit one, cut it 24" wide by 68-3/4" tall from the 1/4" plywood or MDF — that is the full outside footprint of the case including the top, so it covers the back edges rather than sitting inside them. Square the case first, then use the panel to hold it square: line up one corner, tack it, check the diagonal, and only then nail the rest off with 3/4" brads and glue every 6" to 8".
materials: 1/4" plywood or MDF, half sheet (optional back)

steps.title: Add the crown moulding
steps.body: Fit the crown last, after everything else is square and trimmed. Cut the two side returns first, holding each against the piece to mark its length, then fit the front piece between them — a front piece cut slightly long and shaved back closes tighter than one cut to a number. Miter the outside corners at 45° and dry-fit all three pieces together before any glue goes on. Keep the profile modest so it reads as a finished cap rather than an ornament, then glue the miters and pin with 3/4" brads into the header below.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill every nail hole with wood filler and let it dry fully before sanding, or the filler will smear rather than cut. Sand the whole piece at 120 grit to level the filler and the joints, then again at 180 to 220 before finishing, paying particular attention to the mitered crown corners and the leg edges — those are the details people actually look at on a piece like this. Prime and paint, or stain. A tower 68-3/4" tall and only 11-1/4" deep is easy to tip, so anchor it to a wall stud before it gets loaded with books.
materials: Primer and paint, or interior wood stain

images.alt: Tall narrow bookshelf tower with crown moulding
